evidence_quotes.quote: I would like a certain level of protection for customers of video game companies to be established when it comes to games that stop being supported.

There are several types of games that, once support for them ends, would become unplayable despite having been legally bought and in some cases, this can happen for games which were only released a year or so prior to the shutdown.

an end of life plan for video games that makes certain that they are still usable after a company fully moves on from said game is, in my opinion, an important thing to consider for the Digital Fairness Act because I have seen a good few games become unplayable once support has ended and this has made me reconsider several purchases, as I do not want to suddenly lose access to something I have paid for when that can be avoided.

for games that are played offline and only require the internet for leader boards and things like that would only require a small update that disable said leader boards and allow the game to be played without connecting to a server.

in the case of online games, a bit of a bigger update would be needed but even here it would be a relatively small workload as the game would only have to be modified in a way where people who have previously bought the game can host their own servers to continue keeping a given game alive.

paragraphs.text: Hello, I would hereby like to request that the following be considered as a point to be added to the Digital Fairness Act. If possible, I would like a certain level of protection for customers of video game companies to be established when it comes to games that stop being supported. For specific information, it would be best to check out the Stop Killing Games initiative. There is however some misinformation about this campaign out there, so I would like to give a basic rundown of what I would like to see happen. There are several types of games that, once support for them ends, would become unplayable despite having been legally bought and in some cases, this can happen for games which were only released a year or so prior to the shutdown. I do not want to suggest that any and all games are forever supported, that would of course be an insane thing to ask. However, a final update to ensure that such games are still playable is, in my opinion, very much a reasonable request. For games that are played offline and only require the internet for leader boards and things like that would only require a small update that disable said leader boards and allow the game to be played without connecting to a server. In the case of online games, a bit of a bigger update would be needed but even here it would be a relatively small workload as the game would only have to be modified in a way where people who have previously bought the game can host their own servers to continue keeping a given game alive. Either way, an end of life plan for video games that makes certain that they are still usable after a company fully moves on from said game is, in my opinion, an important thing to consider for the Digital Fairness Act because I have seen a good few games become unplayable once support has ended and this has made me reconsider several purchases, as I do not want to suddenly lose access to something I have paid for when that can be avoided. Thank you for your time in reading and considering my request and I hope that you find it to be a reasonable one.
